description.en: Apple scab is a fungal disease caused by Venturia inaequalis. It creates dark, scabby lesions on leaves and fruit, reducing yield and fruit quality significantly.

en: Olive-green or brown spots on leaves
Dark scabby patches on fruit surface
Leaves turn yellow and fall early
Fruit becomes cracked and deformed
Fungal spores spread by rain splash
Cool wet weather in spring
Infected fallen leaves from previous season
Rake and destroy fallen leaves every season
Prune to improve air circulation
Plant scab-resistant varieties
Avoid overhead irrigation

en.title: Neem Oil Spray
en.description: Mix 2 tbsp neem oil in 1 litre water + few drops soap. Spray every 7 days on all leaf surfaces.

en.title: Baking Soda Solution
en.description: Mix 1 tsp baking soda + 1 tsp soap in 1 litre water. Spray weekly as preventive.

en.title: Remove Infected Parts
en.description: Cut and burn all infected leaves and fruit. Do not compost them.

en.title: Mancozeb 75WP
en.description: Spray 2.5g per litre water every 10-14 days during wet season. Start before symptoms appear.

en.title: Captan 50WP
en.description: Apply 2g per litre water. Very effective against scab. Repeat every 7-10 days.

en.title: Thiophanate-methyl
en.description: Apply 1g per litre as systemic fungicide. Use when disease pressure is high.

description.en: Black rot is caused by the fungus Botryosphaeria obtusa. It causes fruit rot, leaf spots, and cankers on branches, leading to major crop loss.

en: Brown circular spots on fruit turning black
Purple-bordered leaf spots
Cankers on branches
Mummified fruit remaining on tree
Fungal infection through wounds
Warm wet weather
Dead wood and mummified fruit as source
Remove all mummified fruit from tree and ground
Prune dead or diseased branches
Avoid injuring bark during pruning
Destroy pruned material — do not leave in orchard

en.title: Copper Spray (Organic)
en.description: Apply copper sulphate + lime (Bordeaux mixture) 1% spray. Apply before bud break and after petal fall.

en.title: Remove Infected Fruit
en.description: Immediately remove and destroy all infected or mummified fruit from tree and ground.

en.title: Pruning
en.description: Cut cankers out of branches at least 15cm below visible infection. Sterilize tools between cuts.

en.title: Thiophanate-methyl 70WP
en.description: Spray 1g per litre at petal fall and repeat every 10-14 days during wet weather.

en.title: Ziram 76WG
en.description: Apply 3g per litre water. Good protectant fungicide for black rot control.

en.title: Captan 50WP
en.description: Apply 2g per litre starting at petal fall. Repeat every 10 days during wet season.

description.en: Cedar apple rust is caused by Gymnosporangium juniperi-virginianae. It requires two hosts — cedar/juniper trees and apple trees — to complete its life cycle.

common_in: Eastern USA, some parts of India

en: Bright orange-yellow spots on upper leaf surface
Tube-like growths on undersides of leaves
Fruit develops orange spots and deforms
Heavy leaf drop in severe cases
Fungal spores spread from nearby cedar/juniper trees
Wet spring weather promotes spore release
Wind carries spores up to several kilometers
Remove cedar/juniper trees near orchard if possible
Plant rust-resistant apple varieties
Apply protective fungicide before and during wet spring weather
Monitor weather forecasts for infection periods

en.title: Sulfur-based Spray
en.description: Apply wettable sulfur 3g per litre water. Start at bud break, repeat every 7-10 days during wet weather.

en.title: Neem Oil
en.description: Apply neem oil 2% spray at first sign of orange spots. Repeat weekly.

en.title: Remove Infected Leaves
en.description: Pick off and destroy heavily infected leaves to reduce local spore load.

en.title: Myclobutanil (Rally 40WP)
en.description: Spray 0.5g per litre water starting at pink bud stage. Very effective against rust fungi.

en.title: Propiconazole
en.description: Apply 1ml per litre water. Systemic fungicide — absorbed by plant and protects from inside.

en.title: Fenarimol
en.description: Apply at first sign of infection. Repeat every 14 days during wet spring.

description.en: Your apple plant appears healthy! Regular monitoring and good agricultural practices will keep it productive and disease-free.

en: Leaves are green and firm with no spots
No discoloration or abnormal growth
Fruit developing normally
Good soil health
Proper watering
No disease pressure
Continue regular monitoring every week
Maintain soil health with compost
Prune annually for good air circulation
Remove fallen leaves and fruit promptly

en.title: Preventive Neem Spray
en.description: Monthly neem oil spray as preventive. Mix 2 tbsp in 1 litre water and spray all leaves.

en.title: Compost Application
en.description: Apply well-composted organic matter around tree base. Improves soil health and resistance.

en.title: Keep Monitoring
en.description: Check leaves every 7 days for any early signs of spots, discoloration, or wilting.

en.title: No Treatment Needed
en.description: Plant is healthy. Avoid unnecessary chemicals to protect beneficial insects.

en.title: Preventive Copper Spray
en.description: Optional: 0.5% Bordeaux mixture spray before monsoon season as broad-spectrum preventive.

en.title: Micronutrient Spray
en.description: Foliar spray of zinc sulphate 0.5% and boron 0.1% once in season for better fruit quality.

description.en: Northern Leaf Blight (NLB) is caused by the fungus Exserohilum turcicum. It is one of the most destructive maize diseases worldwide, including India where it causes 30-60% yield loss.

common_in: High altitude areas with cool humid weather

en: Long cigar-shaped grayish-green lesions on leaves
Lesions turn tan/brown as they mature
Lesions can be 2.5-15cm long
Severe cases: entire plant turns brown and dies
Fungal spores spread by wind and rain
Cool temperatures (18-27°C) with high humidity
Infected crop debris in soil
Susceptible maize varieties
Plant resistant hybrid varieties (HQPM-1, DHM-117)
Deep plowing to bury infected crop debris
Crop rotation with non-host crops
Avoid dense planting — maintain proper spacing
Drain waterlogged fields quickly

en.title: Trichoderma Spray
en.description: Mix Trichoderma viride 4g per litre water. Spray on leaves and drench soil. Biological control — safe and effective.

en.title: Neem Kernel Extract
en.description: Soak 500g neem kernels overnight in 10 litres water. Filter and spray on affected leaves every 10 days.

en.title: Remove Infected Leaves
en.description: Remove and burn heavily infected leaves. Reduces spore load and slows spread.

en.title: Mancozeb 75WP
en.description: Spray 2.5g per litre water at first sign of disease. Repeat every 10-14 days. Most commonly used in India.

en.title: Propiconazole 25EC
en.description: Mix 1ml per litre water. Systemic fungicide — very effective. Apply 2-3 times at 14-day intervals.

en.title: Tebuconazole 25WG
en.description: Apply 1g per litre at early infection stage. Provides both curative and preventive action.

model_labels: corn maize cercospora leaf spot gray leaf spot

description.en: Gray Leaf Spot (GLS) caused by Cercospora zeae-maydis is a major fungal disease of maize. It thrives in humid conditions and causes significant yield loss in Indian maize growing areas.

en: Rectangular gray-tan lesions between leaf veins
Lesions have yellow borders initially
Leaves turn completely brown in severe infection
Lower leaves affected first, moves upward
Cercospora fungus surviving in old crop debris
High humidity and warm nights (21-30°C)
Dew formation on leaves
Dense canopy reducing air flow
Use resistant hybrids (check with KVK)
Deep plow after harvest to bury debris
Proper row spacing for air circulation
Avoid late planting when humidity is high
Rotate crops with soybean or wheat

en.title: Pseudomonas fluorescens Spray
en.description: Mix 10g per litre water. Spray 2-3 times at 15-day intervals. Effective biocontrol agent available at KVK.

en.title: Neem Oil Spray
en.description: Mix 3ml neem oil + 2ml liquid soap in 1 litre water. Spray thoroughly on all leaf surfaces every 7-10 days.

en.title: Baking Soda Spray
en.description: Mix 10g baking soda in 1 litre water with a drop of soap. Spray weekly as preventive measure.

en.title: Azoxystrobin 23SC
en.description: Mix 1ml per litre water. Apply at first sign of disease. Very effective strobilurin fungicide for GLS.

en.title: Propiconazole 25EC
en.description: Apply 1ml per litre water. Provides both protective and curative action. Use 2-3 times at 14-day intervals.

en.title: Chlorothalonil 75WP
en.description: Mix 2g per litre water. Protectant fungicide. Apply preventively every 10-14 days in high risk period.

description.en: Common rust caused by Puccinia sorghi is a widespread maize disease. While rarely causing total crop failure, it can reduce yields by 10-40% if not controlled.

common_in: All maize growing regions of India
More severe in hills and high altitude areas

en: Cinnamon-brown pustules on both leaf surfaces
Pustules are oval to elongated, 1-2mm
Heavy infection causes yellowing and leaf death
Pustules turn dark brown-black late in season
Airborne fungal spores from distant sources
Cool temperatures (16-25°C)
High humidity and frequent rains
Susceptible varieties
Plant rust-resistant hybrid varieties
Avoid late planting in cool season
Monitor crop weekly during humid weather
Remove and burn severely infected plants

en.title: Sulfur Dust
en.description: Dust wettable sulfur 20-25kg per hectare at first appearance of rust. Repeat after 10 days. Effective and cheap.

en.title: Neem Oil Spray
en.description: Spray 2% neem oil solution every 7-10 days. Reduces spore germination and disease spread.

en.title: Trichoderma Application
en.description: Apply Trichoderma viride as soil drench 4g per litre water to boost plant immunity.

en.title: Mancozeb 75WP
en.description: Spray 2.5g per litre water at first sign. Repeat every 10-14 days. Most economical option.

en.title: Propiconazole 25EC
en.description: Apply 1ml per litre water. Controls rust quickly. One or two sprays usually sufficient.

en.title: Tebuconazole + Trifloxystrobin
en.description: Combination fungicide — apply 0.5g per litre. Excellent control of all maize rust diseases.

description.en: Your maize plant appears healthy! Keep up the good work. Continue monitoring regularly to catch any early disease signs.

en: Dark green healthy leaves
No spots, lesions or discoloration
Strong upright growth
Good soil and crop management
Adequate nutrition and water
Continue weekly monitoring
Maintain balanced NPK fertilization
Control weeds that harbor pests
Ensure proper drainage in fields

en.title: Preventive Neem Spray
en.description: Monthly preventive spray of 2% neem oil to keep diseases and pests away.

en.title: Compost Application
en.description: Apply well-composted FYM (Farm Yard Manure) at 10 tonnes per hectare for soil health.

en.title: Monitoring
en.description: Inspect at least 10 plants per field weekly. Check both sides of leaves for early symptoms.

en.title: No Treatment Needed
en.description: Plant is healthy. Focus on soil nutrition and timely irrigation.

en.title: Preventive Mancozeb Spray
en.description: Optional: 0.2% Mancozeb spray before monsoon as broad-spectrum preventive.

en.title: Micronutrient Application
en.description: Spray zinc sulphate 0.5% at knee-high stage for better yield and disease resistance.

description.en: Early Blight caused by Alternaria solani is one of the most common tomato diseases in India. It can cause 50-80% yield loss if untreated. Common in Karnataka, Maharashtra and Andhra Pradesh.

en: Brown spots with yellow halos on lower leaves first
Target-like concentric rings inside leaf spots
Fruit develops dark leathery patches near stem
Spots enlarge and leaves turn yellow and drop
Alternaria fungus surviving in soil and old debris
Warm temperatures 24-29°C with high humidity
Overhead irrigation wetting leaves
Stressed or nitrogen-deficient plants
Rotate crops — avoid tomato in same field 2-3 years
Avoid wetting leaves — water at base only
Mulch soil to prevent soil splash
Ensure good plant spacing for air circulation
Remove lower infected leaves immediately

en.title: Copper Bordeaux Spray
en.description: Mix 100g copper sulphate + 100g lime in 10 litres water. Spray every 10 days. Safe and very effective.

en.title: Neem Oil Spray
en.description: Mix 2 tbsp neem oil + 1 tsp soap in 1 litre water. Spray all leaf surfaces every 7 days.

en.title: Baking Soda Spray
en.description: Mix 1 tbsp baking soda in 1 litre water + soap. Spray weekly. Changes leaf surface pH to prevent fungal growth.

en.title: Remove Infected Leaves
en.description: Remove all spotted leaves immediately and burn. Never compost infected plant material.

en.title: Mancozeb 75WP
en.description: Spray 2.5g per litre water every 7-10 days. Preventive fungicide — start before disease appears in rainy season.

en.title: Chlorothalonil 75WP
en.description: Mix 2g per litre water. Excellent contact fungicide for early blight. Apply every 7-14 days.

en.title: Iprodione 50WP
en.description: Apply 2g per litre for curative action when infection is already present. 2-3 applications needed.

en.title: Azoxystrobin 23SC
en.description: Mix 1ml per litre water. Systemic — absorbed by plant. Highly effective, use when disease is spreading fast.

description.en: Late Blight caused by Phytophthora infestans is an extremely destructive disease. It can destroy entire crops within days in humid conditions. The same disease caused the Irish Potato Famine.

en: Water-soaked dark brown patches on leaves
White fuzzy growth on leaf undersides in early morning
Stem lesions turn dark brown and collapse
Fruit develops large firm brown rot from outside
Phytophthora water mold spreading in rain
Cool temperatures 10-25°C with high humidity
Overhead irrigation or rain keeping leaves wet
Rapidly spreads in dense plantings
Never plant tomato where late blight occurred last season
Avoid overhead irrigation — use drip irrigation
Space plants well — minimum 60cm apart
Remove and destroy first infected plants immediately
Monitor weather — spray before rain if blight expected

en.title: Copper Sulphate Spray
en.description: Apply 0.5% copper sulphate solution immediately at first sign. Spray every 5-7 days. Critical — do not delay.

en.title: Bordeaux Mixture
en.description: Prepare 1% Bordeaux mixture (100g copper sulphate + 100g lime per 10 litres). Apply every 7-10 days preventively.

en.title: Remove Infected Plants
en.description: Remove and burn infected plants same day. Do NOT leave in field or compost. One infected plant can destroy entire crop.

en.title: Metalaxyl + Mancozeb
en.description: Apply 2.5g per litre water. Systemic + contact action. Best option for late blight control in India.

en.title: Cymoxanil + Mancozeb
en.description: Mix 2g per litre water. Apply every 7 days during blight-favorable weather. Curative and preventive.

en.title: Dimethomorph 50WP
en.description: Apply 1g per litre. Excellent against Phytophthora. Use when infection has already started.

description.en: Leaf Mold caused by Passalora fulva (formerly Fulvia fulva) mainly affects greenhouse/protected tomatoes. It causes yellowing and premature leaf drop, reducing photosynthesis and yield.

en: Yellow patches on upper leaf surface
Olive-green to gray fuzzy mold on underside of leaves
Infected leaves curl upward and drop
Severe cases: entire plant defoliates
Fungal spores spread in humid air
Humidity above 85% for prolonged periods
Poor ventilation in greenhouse or dense planting
Warm temperatures 21-24°C with high humidity
Improve ventilation in greenhouse — open vents
Reduce humidity by spacing plants properly
Avoid overhead watering
Remove lower leaves to improve air flow
Plant resistant varieties where available

en.title: Improve Air Circulation
en.description: Open greenhouse vents fully. Remove 2-3 lower leaves per plant. This alone can stop mild infections.

en.title: Copper Spray
en.description: Apply 0.5% copper sulphate or Bordeaux mixture. Spray undersides of leaves thoroughly every 10 days.

en.title: Neem Oil + Garlic Spray
en.description: Mix 2 tbsp neem oil + 1 tbsp crushed garlic extract in 1 litre water. Spray undersides of leaves every 7 days.

en.title: Chlorothalonil 75WP
en.description: Spray 2g per litre water on undersides of leaves. Apply every 7-10 days. Good protective fungicide.

en.title: Mancozeb 75WP
en.description: Mix 2.5g per litre. Apply every 10-14 days preventively before humid season begins.

en.title: Difenoconazole 25EC
en.description: Apply 0.5ml per litre water for curative action. 2-3 applications at 10-day intervals.

description.en: Septoria leaf spot caused by Septoria lycopersici is a very common tomato disease in India. It starts on lower leaves and moves upward, causing severe defoliation.

en: Small circular spots with dark borders and light centers
Tiny black specks (spore bodies) visible in spot centers
Lower leaves affected first
Rapid spread upward in wet weather
Leaves yellow, wither and drop
Fungal spores in infected soil and plant debris
Warm temperatures 20-25°C
Rain splash spreading spores to leaves
Overhead irrigation
Mulch soil to prevent rain splash
Remove lower leaves touching soil
Rotate crops — avoid tomato same field 3 years
Use disease-free seeds
Stake plants to improve air circulation

en.title: Copper Bordeaux Spray
en.description: Apply 1% Bordeaux mixture every 10 days starting at first sign. Very effective against Septoria.

en.title: Neem Oil Spray
en.description: Mix 2% neem oil with soap emulsifier. Spray every 7-10 days — especially on lower leaves.

en.title: Remove Infected Leaves
en.description: Remove all spotted leaves from bottom. Wash hands after touching. Reduces spread significantly.

en.title: Chlorothalonil 75WP
en.description: Spray 2g per litre water. Start early in season before infection appears. Highly effective.

en.title: Mancozeb 75WP
en.description: Mix 2.5g per litre water. Apply every 7-10 days during wet weather. Standard treatment in India.

en.title: Thiophanate-methyl 70WP
en.description: Apply 1g per litre for systemic action. Use when disease is spreading rapidly.

description.en: Bacterial Spot caused by Xanthomonas vesicatoria affects both leaves and fruit. It is difficult to control once established and can cause 10-50% fruit loss.

en: Small water-soaked spots on leaves turning brown
Spots have yellow halo around them
Raised scabby lesions on fruit
Lesions crack open in dry weather
Severe defoliation in humid conditions
Bacterial infection through leaf openings and wounds
Warm temperatures 25-30°C with high humidity
Seed-borne infection
Rain and wind spreading bacteria
Contaminated tools and workers
Use certified disease-free seeds
Avoid working in field when plants are wet
Sanitize tools with 10% bleach solution
Rotate crops for 2-3 years
Remove infected plants immediately

en.title: Copper Hydroxide Spray
en.description: Apply copper hydroxide 50WP at 3g per litre water. Spray every 7-10 days. Best organic option for bacterial diseases.

en.title: Neem + Garlic Spray
en.description: Mix 2% neem oil + 2% garlic extract in water. Antibacterial properties help slow spread.

en.title: Remove Infected Material
en.description: Remove infected leaves and fruit. Wash hands with soap after contact. Sanitize tools with bleach.

en.title: Copper Oxychloride 50WP
en.description: Mix 3g per litre water. Apply every 7-10 days. Standard treatment for bacterial diseases — widely available in India.

en.title: Streptomycin Sulphate + Copper
en.description: Mix streptomycin 500ppm + copper oxychloride 0.3%. Apply in early morning every 10 days.

en.title: Kasugamycin 3SL
en.description: Apply 2ml per litre water. Antibiotic fungicide effective against bacterial spot. 2-3 applications needed.

model_labels: tomato spider mites two-spotted spider mite

description.en: Two-spotted spider mites (Tetranychus urticae) are tiny pests that suck plant sap from leaf undersides, causing stippling, yellowing and in severe cases, complete plant death.

en: Tiny yellow or white stippling/dots on upper leaf surface
Fine webbing visible on underside of leaves
Leaves turn bronze, yellow then brown
Tiny moving dots visible under magnifying glass
Dry crinkled leaves in severe infestation
Hot dry conditions (above 30°C, low humidity)
Overcrowding of plants
Dusty field conditions
Broad-spectrum pesticide use killing natural predators
Maintain adequate moisture — mites hate humid conditions
Spray water on leaf undersides to dislodge mites
Avoid broad-spectrum pesticides that kill predatory mites
Introduce predatory mites (Phytoseiidae) as biocontrol
Keep field edges weed-free

en.title: Strong Water Spray
en.description: Spray water forcefully on leaf undersides daily for 3-5 days. Simple but very effective for mild infestations.

en.title: Neem Oil Spray
en.description: Mix 2% neem oil + 0.5% soap in water. Spray undersides of leaves every 5-7 days. Kills mites and eggs.

en.title: Onion-Garlic Spray
en.description: Blend 100g onion + 50g garlic in 1 litre water. Filter and spray undiluted on leaf undersides. Natural miticide.

en.title: Abamectin 1.9EC
en.description: Mix 1ml per litre water. Very effective miticide. 2 applications at 7-day interval usually sufficient.

en.title: Spiromesifen 22.9SC
en.description: Apply 1ml per litre water. Excellent for two-spotted spider mite. Controls all life stages.

en.title: Propargite 57EC
en.description: Mix 2ml per litre water. Contact miticide. Spray thoroughly on undersides of leaves.

description.en: Target Spot caused by Corynespora cassiicola is an emerging disease of tomato in India. It causes significant defoliation and fruit spotting.

en: Circular brown spots with concentric ring pattern (target-like)
Yellow halo around spots
Fruit develops sunken dark spots
Rapid spread in warm humid conditions
Fungal infection in warm humid weather
Infected plant debris
Rain and wind spreading spores
Use resistant varieties
Crop rotation every 2-3 years
Remove infected leaves promptly
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization

en.title: Copper Spray
en.description: Apply 1% Bordeaux mixture or copper hydroxide every 10 days. Start preventively in humid season.

en.title: Neem Oil Spray
en.description: 2% neem oil spray every 7-10 days provides moderate control.

en.title: Remove Infected Leaves
en.description: Regularly remove spotted leaves to slow disease progress.

en.title: Azoxystrobin 23SC
en.description: Apply 1ml per litre water. Excellent systemic action. 2-3 applications at 10-14 day intervals.

en.title: Boscalid + Pyraclostrobin
en.description: Apply 1g per litre. Premium fungicide combination — excellent control of target spot.

en.title: Chlorothalonil 75WP
en.description: Mix 2g per litre water as protective spray every 10-14 days.

model_labels: tomato tomato yellow leaf curl virus

description.en: Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus (TYLCV) is one of the most devastating tomato diseases in India. It is transmitted by whiteflies and has no chemical cure — only management through vector control.

en: Leaves curl upward with yellow edges
Leaves become small and crinkled
Stunted plant growth
Very few or no fruits formed
Veins on underside appear purple
Begomovirus spread exclusively by Bemisia tabaci (silverleaf whitefly)
No other transmission method
Whiteflies migrate from infected fields
No resistant varieties widely available in India
Use yellow sticky traps to catch whiteflies
Install 50-mesh insect-proof net houses for nurseries
Apply reflective mulch to repel whiteflies
Remove and destroy infected plants immediately
Do not plant near infected fields

en.title: Remove Infected Plants
en.description: Remove infected plants immediately and bury or burn them. Keeping infected plants in field is the main source of spread.

en.title: Yellow Sticky Traps
en.description: Install 15-20 yellow sticky traps per acre. Catches adult whiteflies and monitors population. Hang at plant height.

en.title: Neem Oil Spray for Whitefly
en.description: Mix 3% neem oil + soap in water. Spray on undersides of leaves every 5 days to kill whitefly nymphs.

en.title: Imidacloprid 17.8SL
en.description: Mix 0.5ml per litre water. Spray on undersides of leaves to kill whiteflies. No direct effect on virus.

en.title: Thiamethoxam 25WG
en.description: Apply 0.3g per litre as soil drench or foliar spray. Systemic insecticide controlling whitefly effectively.

en.title: Spiromesifen 22.9SC
en.description: Mix 1ml per litre water. Controls whitefly nymphs. Rotate with other insecticides to prevent resistance.

description.en: Tomato Mosaic Virus (ToMV) is a mechanically transmitted virus causing mosaic patterns, distortion and yield loss. Very stable — can survive in dry soil for 2 years.

en: Yellow-green mosaic pattern on leaves
Leaves distorted, curled or fernleaf appearance
Stunted plant with reduced fruit set
Fruit may show yellow streaks or rings
Plants infected early: no fruit at all
Mechanical transmission — touching infected then healthy plant
Seed-borne — infected seeds carry virus
Spread by workers, tools, grafting
No insect vector — spreads by touch
Use virus-free certified seeds
Dip seeds in 10% trisodium phosphate for 15 min before sowing
Wash hands with soap before working with plants
Sterilize tools with bleach or fire
Remove infected plants immediately

en.title: Remove Infected Plants
en.description: Remove and bury infected plants. Wear gloves. No cure exists — only prevent spread.

en.title: Milk Spray
en.description: Spray 10% milk solution on healthy plants. Research shows milk proteins inhibit Tobamovirus transmission.

en.title: Hand Hygiene
en.description: Wash hands with soap for 30 seconds before touching plants. Do not touch plants after handling infected material.

en.title: No Curative Treatment
en.description: No chemical can cure virus infection. Focus on prevention and removing infected plants.

en.title: Seed Treatment
en.description: Soak seeds in 10% trisodium phosphate solution for 15 minutes before planting. Kills seed-borne virus.

en.title: Tool Sterilization
en.description: Use 1% sodium hypochlorite (bleach) solution to sterilize pruning tools between plants. Prevents spread.

description.en: Your tomato plant appears healthy! Continue good agricultural practices to maintain this health throughout the season.

en: Deep green leaves with no spots
Strong upright stems
Flowers and fruit forming normally
Good crop management
Balanced nutrition
Proper irrigation
Water at base — avoid wetting leaves
Check weekly for early disease signs
Maintain NPK fertilization schedule
Control weeds around plants

en.title: Preventive Neem Spray
en.description: Monthly neem oil spray as preventive against fungal diseases and pests.

en.title: Compost Tea
en.description: Apply diluted compost tea as foliar spray monthly. Adds beneficial microbes that protect against disease.

en.title: Mulching
en.description: Maintain organic mulch layer around plants. Prevents soil splash which spreads many diseases.

en.title: No Treatment Needed
en.description: Plant is healthy. No chemicals needed.

en.title: Preventive Copper Spray
en.description: Optional: 0.5% copper spray before monsoon season as broad preventive measure.

en.title: Micronutrient Foliar Spray
en.description: Calcium nitrate 0.5% spray during fruit development improves fruit quality and shelf life.

description.en: Early Blight caused by Alternaria solani is a major potato disease in India. Same pathogen as tomato early blight. Very common in UP, Bihar, West Bengal during monsoon.

common_in: Potato fields in UP, Bihar, WB

en: Dark brown spots with yellow halo on older lower leaves
Target-like rings inside leaf spots
Spots enlarge and merge — leaf dies
Tubers show dark sunken lesions at harvest
Alternaria fungus in infected soil and debris
Warm temperatures 24-29°C
Wet weather and overhead irrigation
Plants under nutrient stress most susceptible
Use certified disease-free seed potatoes
Rotate crop — no potato same field for 3 years
Avoid nitrogen deficiency
Remove and destroy infected leaves
Earthing up reduces tuber infection

en.title: Bordeaux Mixture
en.description: Apply 1% Bordeaux mixture every 10-14 days preventively. Start at crop emergence.

en.title: Neem Oil Spray
en.description: 2% neem oil spray every 7 days. Moderately effective against early blight.

en.title: Remove Infected Foliage
en.description: Cut and remove infected leaves. Reduces spore load and slows spread.

en.title: Mancozeb 75WP
en.description: Mix 2.5g per litre water. Apply every 7-10 days during vulnerable period. Standard treatment in India.

en.title: Chlorothalonil 75WP
en.description: Apply 2g per litre. Excellent protective fungicide. Start early before disease appears.

en.title: Iprodione 50WP
en.description: Apply 2g per litre for curative action when disease is already present.

description.en: Potato Late Blight caused by Phytophthora infestans is the most destructive potato disease worldwide. In India it devastates crops in hill regions and causes major losses in plains during cool humid weather.

en: Water-soaked dark lesions on leaves at margins or tips
White fuzzy mold on undersides in early morning
Stems develop dark brown rot and collapse
Tubers show reddish-brown rot from skin inward
Disease spreads extremely fast — field can collapse in 3-5 days
Phytophthora water mold
Cool temperatures 10-20°C with rain or heavy dew
Infected tubers used as seed
Diseased volunteer potato plants
Use certified disease-free seed potatoes
Never use tubers from infected fields as seed
Apply preventive fungicide before blight-favorable weather
Plant resistant varieties (Kufri Jyoti, Kufri Bahar)
Destroy haulms (tops) before harvest to prevent tuber infection

en.title: Copper Sulphate Spray
en.description: Apply 0.5% copper sulphate immediately at first sign. Spray every 5-7 days. Critical to act fast.

en.title: Bordeaux Mixture 1%
en.description: Apply every 7 days preventively in cool humid weather. Classic and effective treatment.

en.title: Remove Infected Plants
en.description: Remove entire infected plant including roots immediately. Do not leave in field — one plant can infect entire crop.

en.title: Metalaxyl + Mancozeb (Ridomil Gold)
en.description: Mix 2.5g per litre water. Best option — systemic + contact. Apply every 7 days in high-risk weather.

en.title: Dimethomorph 50WP
en.description: Apply 1g per litre water. Excellent systemic fungicide. Use when late blight has started.

en.title: Cymoxanil + Mancozeb
en.description: Mix 2g per litre. Apply every 7 days. Good curative and preventive action.

description.en: Your potato plant appears healthy! Maintain good practices to ensure a good harvest.

en: Green healthy foliage
No spots or lesions
Normal growth
Good crop management
Monitor weekly especially in humid weather
Apply preventive fungicide before monsoon
Ensure good drainage in field

en.title: Preventive Bordeaux Spray
en.description: Apply 0.5% Bordeaux mixture monthly as preventive in monsoon season.

en.title: Earthing Up
en.description: Earth up well around plants to protect tubers and improve drainage.

en.title: Compost Application
en.description: Apply FYM 15-20 tonnes per hectare for soil health and disease resistance.

en.title: No Treatment Needed
en.description: Plant is healthy.

en.title: Preventive Mancozeb Spray
en.description: Optional: 0.25% Mancozeb spray weekly during monsoon as preventive against late blight.

en.title: Micronutrient Application
en.description: Boron 0.1% + zinc 0.5% foliar spray at tuber initiation stage improves yield.

description.en: Grape Black Rot caused by Guignardia bidwellii causes fruit and leaf lesions. A major disease in Indian vineyards, especially in Maharashtra (Nashik region).

en: Small reddish-brown circular spots on leaves
Fruit develops reddish spots, shrivels to hard black mummy
Lesions on shoots with dark margins
Berries completely lost in severe infection
Fungal infection through rain splash
Warm wet weather during berry development
Mummified fruit as primary inoculum source
Remove all mummified fruit from vine and ground
Prune for good air circulation
Apply protective fungicide before rain
Destroy prunings — do not leave in vineyard

en.title: Bordeaux Mixture
en.description: Apply 1% Bordeaux mixture at bud break and repeat every 10-14 days through berry development.

en.title: Remove Mummies
en.description: Remove all mummified berries from vine before new season. Major source of infection.

en.title: Sulfur Dust
en.description: Apply sulfur dust 20-25kg per hectare. Reduces spore germination.

en.title: Myclobutanil 10WP
en.description: Apply 1g per litre water. Excellent systemic fungicide for grape diseases. Apply preventively.

en.title: Mancozeb 75WP
en.description: Mix 2.5g per litre water. Apply every 10-14 days as contact protectant.

en.title: Fludioxonil + Cyprodinil
en.description: Apply as per label. Premium combination fungicide for grape disease management.

description.en: Citrus Greening (Huanglongbing/HLB) caused by Candidatus Liberibacter asiaticus is the most devastating citrus disease worldwide. Transmitted by Asian citrus psyllid. No cure exists.

common_in: Citrus orchards in Maharashtra, Karnataka, AP

en: Asymmetric yellowing of leaves (blotchy mottle)
Leaves small, leathery and upright
Fruit remains green, misshapen and bitter
Tree slowly declines over years
Shoots die back from tips
Bacterial infection transmitted by Asian citrus psyllid (Diaphorina citri)
Grafting from infected trees
No other transmission method
Use certified disease-free grafted plants only
Control citrus psyllid population rigorously
Remove and destroy infected trees immediately
Do not bring planting material from unknown sources
Survey orchard monthly for psyllid and symptoms

en.title: Remove Infected Trees
en.description: Remove and destroy infected trees immediately. They cannot be cured and will infect neighbors.

en.title: Neem Oil for Psyllid
en.description: Spray 3% neem oil every 5-7 days to control psyllid population. Focus on new flush growth where psyllid breeds.

en.title: Sticky Yellow Traps
en.description: Install yellow sticky traps to monitor and trap adult psyllids. 20-25 traps per acre.

en.title: Imidacloprid 17.8SL for Psyllid
en.description: Apply 0.5ml per litre water. Spray on new flush to kill psyllids before they transmit disease.

en.title: Dimethoate 30EC
en.description: Mix 2ml per litre water. Apply every 2-3 weeks during flush growth. Controls psyllid adults and nymphs.

en.title: No Cure for Tree
en.description: No chemical cures HLB. Management focuses entirely on killing the psyllid vector.

description.en: Bacterial Spot of pepper caused by Xanthomonas euvesicatoria causes serious leaf drop and fruit lesions. Common in capsicum growing regions of India.

en: Small water-soaked spots on leaves turning dark brown
Yellow halo surrounding spots
Raised scabby spots on fruit
Severe defoliation in wet weather
Bacterial infection through leaf pores and wounds
Warm wet weather
Seed-borne bacteria
Spread by rain splash and tools
Use certified disease-free seeds
Avoid overhead irrigation
Rotate crops for 2-3 years
Sanitize tools regularly
Remove infected plants promptly

en.title: Copper Hydroxide Spray
en.description: Apply 3g per litre water every 7-10 days. Most effective organic option for bacterial diseases.

en.title: Neem-Garlic Spray
en.description: Mix 2% neem oil + 2% garlic extract. Spray every 7 days for antibacterial effect.

en.title: Remove Infected Leaves
en.description: Remove spotted leaves and burn. Wash hands with soap after handling infected material.

en.title: Copper Oxychloride 50WP
en.description: Mix 3g per litre water. Apply every 7-10 days. Standard treatment for bacterial spot.

en.title: Streptomycin + Copper
en.description: 500ppm streptomycin + 0.3% copper oxychloride. Apply every 10 days. Effective combination.

en.title: Kasugamycin 3SL
en.description: Apply 2ml per litre. Antibiotic mode of action. 2-3 applications needed.

description.en: Leaf Scorch caused by Diplocarpon earlianum is a common strawberry disease causing reddish-purple spots and leaf death. Reduces plant vigour and fruit production.

en: Irregular reddish-purple spots on leaf surface
Centers of spots turn light brown or gray
Leaves look scorched in severe cases
Spots do not have halos unlike other diseases
Fungal infection in wet conditions
Cool wet weather
Overhead irrigation wetting foliage
High planting density
Use drip irrigation — avoid wetting leaves
Remove infected leaves promptly
Improve air circulation by proper spacing
Plant certified disease-free runners

en.title: Bordeaux Mixture
en.description: Apply 0.5% Bordeaux mixture every 10-14 days during wet season.

en.title: Neem Oil
en.description: Spray 2% neem oil solution weekly to slow disease spread.

en.title: Remove Infected Leaves
en.description: Remove all infected leaves and burn. Significantly reduces spore load.

en.title: Captan 50WP
en.description: Apply 2g per litre water every 10 days. Most commonly recommended for strawberry leaf diseases.

en.title: Myclobutanil 10WP
en.description: Apply 1g per litre for systemic control. 2-3 applications at 14-day intervals.

en.title: Thiophanate-methyl 70WP
en.description: Apply 1g per litre for curative action when disease is already present.
